coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of ultrasound and microbubbles effect on pork meat during brining process
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Visy, Anna
Jónás, Gábor
Szakos, Dávid
Horváth-Mezőfi, Zsuzsanna
Hidas, Karina Ilona
Barkó, Annamária
Friedrich, László
coredata.dc:description: In this study combined effect of ultrasound-induced acoustic cavitation and microbubbles during meat brining on pork loin (Longissimus dorsi) was evaluated. Cylindrical shape (diameter 15 mm, height 80 mm) pork loin samples were cut and immersed in 200 g L-1 NaCl brine and treated with the following brining methods for 180 min: static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubbles in brine (USMB). Ultrasound was generated with 20 kHz frequency, 5,09 W/cm2 maximum intensity and 100 W maximum power. Microbubbles in brine were produced by a gas–liquid mixing pump. Effect of ultrasound and microbbubles on NaCl content and diffusion in pork loin, mass balance, water binding capacity (WBC), protein denaturation and meat tissue microstructure were evaluated. The US and USMB brinings enhanced the NaCl diffusion into meat compared to meat brined under static conditions. The constant diffusion coefficient (D) model precisely described the NaCl diffusion kinetics during brinings. The ultrasound and microbbubles resulted in microscopic pores on the surface of myofibers. Decreasing WBC was observed for all brining methods. Myosin was not detectable in any of the brining methods. Denaturation temperature of actin showed a decreasing tendency with increasing brining time independently the brining methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ultrasound
Microbubble
Cavitation
Brining
NaCl
Diffusion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ultrasound and microbubble generation meat brining system (MB – microbubble)

Changing of NaCl mass transfer (mNaCl/M0) in case of static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine (USMB) on cylinder shaped pork loin (d = 15 mm; h = 80 mm). Data points represent the average of three measurements and error bars show the standard deviations.

Changing of water mass transfer (mwater/M0) in case of static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine (USMB) on cylinder shaped pork loin (d = 15 mm; h = 80 mm). Data points represent the average of three measurements and error bars show the standard deviations.

Changing of NaCl content in case of static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine (USMB) on cylinder shaped pork loin (d = 15 mm; h = 80 mm). Data points represent the average of three measurements and error bars show the standard deviations.

Diffusion kinetics of NaCl from 200 g L-1 brine into cylinder shaped pork loin (d = 15 mm; h = 80 mm) as a function of different brining methods static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine (USMB). Data points represent the average of three measurements and error bars show the standard deviations.

Changes in water binding capacity (WBC) of pork loin in case of static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine (USMB). Data points represent the average of three measurements and error bars show the standard deviations.

SEM micrographs of pork loins as a function of different treatments after 180-minute brining a) static brining (SB); b) ultrasound assisted brining (US); c) ultrasound combined with microbubble brining (USMB). Black circles show the small pores on USMB pork loin.
Calculated diffusion coefficients (D) of NaCl in cylinder shaped pork loin (d = 15 mm; h = 80 mm) for various brining methods.

row.entry: Ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine brining (USMB)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Influence of different brining methods on maximum denaturation temperature (Tmax,°C) of myosin and actin. SB static brining, US ultrasound assisted brining, USMB ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine. Data expressed in average of three measurements ± standard deviation; n.d. not detectable.

row.entry: Maximum denaturation temperature of myosin (Tmax, myosin) (°C)
Maximum denaturation temperature of actin (Tmax, actin) (°C)

head.ce:title: Evaluation of ultrasound and microbubbles effect on pork meat during brining process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Hungarian University of Agriculture and Life Sciences, Ménesi út 43-45, 1118 Budapest, Hungary

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food Science and Technology
Hungarian University of Agriculture and Life Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Food Science and Technology, Hungarian University of Agriculture and Life Sciences, Ménesi út 43-45, 1118 Budapest, Hungary

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Veterinary Medicine Budapest, István u. 2, 1078 Budapest, Hungary
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Veterinary Medicine Budapest, István u. 2, 1078 Budapest, Hungary

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study combined effect of ultrasound-induced acoustic cavitation and microbubbles during meat brining on pork loin (Longissimus dorsi) was evaluated. Cylindrical shape (diameter 15 mm, height 80 mm) pork loin samples were cut and immersed in 200 g L-1 NaCl brine and treated with the following brining methods for 180 min: static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubbles in brine (USMB). Ultrasound was generated with 20 kHz frequency, 5,09 W/cm2 maximum intensity and 100 W maximum power. Microbubbles in brine were produced by a gas–liquid mixing pump. Effect of ultrasound and microbbubles on NaCl content and diffusion in pork loin, mass balance, water binding capacity (WBC), protein denaturation and meat tissue microstructure were evaluated. The US and USMB brinings enhanced the NaCl diffusion into meat compared to meat brined under static conditions. The constant diffusion coefficient (D) model precisely described the NaCl diffusion kinetics during brinings. The ultrasound and microbbubles resulted in microscopic pores on the surface of myofibers. Decreasing WBC was observed for all brining methods. Myosin was not detectable in any of the brining methods. Denaturation temperature of actin showed a decreasing tendency with increasing brining time independently the brining methods.

ce:section.ce:para: Brining or curing is one of the oldest preservation methods for meat. It is based on the diffusion of NaCl into the meat. The NaCl results in physico-chemical and biochemical changes in meat, enhances shelf-life, flavour, juiciness and tenderness of meat products[1,2].

Common to all curing methods is that NaCl diffuses through the meat matrix and the diffusion rate depends on the local concentration gradient. The diffusion of NaCl into the meat under normal conditions (e.g. brining, salting) is usually slow due to the complex matrix of meat and also prevented by the inter- and intramuscular fat between myofibrils[3,4]) . Therefore curing process is often complemented with other treatments, such as tumbling and injecting of the meat as mechanical treatments, which causes cellular disruption of the meat tissue[3,5]. These methods allow the NaCl to penetrate into the meat tissue more quickly but they are not be used in traditional dry cured ham’s production.

The high intensity ultrasound also known as power ultrasound is a promising method to increase the NaCl diffusion in meat immersed in brine and is used in the food industry for accelerating brining processes and enhancing mass transfer[6,7]), [2,8,9,10]. Moreover, power ultrasound acting on meat texture and providing a better distribution of salt in the meat matrix could be helpful in the development of reduced salt meat formulations[11]. The acceleration of mass transfer has been attributed to several ultrasound mechanisms, notably cavitation, which is the implosion of microscopic gas bubbles due to sound wave propagation at frequencies greater than 20 kHz[2,4].

Cavitation is classified into four types based on mode of generation: acoustic, hydrodynamic, optic and particle cavitation. Only acoustic and hydrodynamic cavitation can cause chemical and/or physical changes, whereas optic and particle cavitation are typically used for single bubble cavitation, which fails to induce any physical and/or chemical change in materials[12]. Acoustic cavitation is the growth and collapse of vapour bubbles embedded in liquid medium. In a steady state, with high acoustic intensity and increased energy in liquid medium, a change in state takes place due to elevated temperature and the phenomenon occurs when pressure in a fluid decreases rapidly, creating micron-size bubbles; then, when the fluid pressure increases again, these microbubbles implode[13,14]. Prior to microbubble implosion, the pulsating actions of bubble growth through sound wave cycles may cause acoustic streaming which ensures agitation of fluid enhancing the mass transfer during meat curing or brining[2]. Besides that implosion of cavities produce shock-waves and microjets that cause biological tissue damage leading to further increased mass transfer into the meat[4].

Sonoporation refers to a process where ultrasound is used to enhance membrane permeability of cells[15,16]) . Cavitating microbubbles have mechanical, chemical and shear stress effect on cell membrane that leads to the formation of pores in the cell membrane and allows molecules to enter the cell via passive diffusion[17]), [18,19,20;21]) . In the field of drug delivery several studies investigated the use of microbubbles to amplify the biophysical effects of ultrasound. Generally microbubbles are gas-filled structures stabilized by protein, lipid, polymer shell or unshelled and some of them are clinically approved as ultrasound agents[22,23]. Dijkink et al.[24]and Ohl et al.[25]investigated the effect of unshelled microbubbles and their findings showed that imploding microbubbles generate liquid jets that have capacity to puncture cell surfaces and create membrane pores. It has been suggested that the liquid microjets are able to deliver drugs to cells[26]. Therefore using ultrasound combined with microbubble caused more jets and seems to be a promising way to further enhance NaCl transfer though the muscle membrane during brining.

The effect of ultrasound-induced acoustic cavitation is known during meat curing but no information is yet available on the combined effect of ultrasound and microbubbles. Therefore the aim of our study is to investigate the combined effect of ultrasound-induced acoustic cavitation and microbubbles on pork meat during brining.

Pork loin (Longissimus dorsi) obtained from a local slaughterhouse was used for all investigations. Pork loin was chosen as it can be considered as a relatively homogenous muscle. Cylinder shaped meat samples (diameter (d) = 15 mm, height (h) = 80 mm) were cut from the meat parallel to the longitudinal axis of the meat fibers. Weight of samples were 5.0 g ± 0.13 g and pH 5.82 ± 0.12.

In our present study three types of brining methods were evaluated and compared to each other: static brining (SB), ultrasound assisted brining (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine (USMB). In each brining method 21 cylindrical shape samples were immersed in brine containing 200 g L-1 NaCl for 15, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150 and 180 min, respectively and three samples were taken out at given time to analyse. The sampling was randomized to avoid the effect of the possible uneven energy distribution. A total of 63 samples were analysed in the experiment. The meat samples were placed on a grid structure so that they were not in contact with each other, thus ensuring the maximum free surface to be in contact with the brine. During each brining temperature was kept at 8 °C by controlling the temperature of the ultrasound probe and ambient air. Ratio of meat samples and brine (P) was 1:500 for all brinings.
Meat cylinders were placed into a steel tank filled with 200 g L-1 NaCl brine and soaked without any agitation.

The ultrasonic probe was placed at the bottom of a steel tank filled with 200 g L-1 brine. Ultrasound was generated by Power Ultrasound System (Ultrasonotech Team, Mosonmagyarovar, Hungary) equipment with 20 kHz frequency, 5,09 W/cm2 maximum intensity and 100 W maximum power. The ultrasonic probe was cooled by a water cooling system. Meat samples were arranged without direct contact with ultrasonic probe and muscle fibers were perpendicular to the ultrasonic probe. Direct agitation in brine was not used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasound-induced acoustic cavitation combined with microbubbles brining
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrasound and microbubble generation system is shown in Fig. 1 . The microbubbles (MB) in brine were produced with ambient air by a gas–liquid mixing pump (Type: YL8022, model: 25GO-2SS, 1.1 kW, Guangzhou Ozone Environmental Technology Co. GZ, China) in continuous operation with pressure of 0.5–0.6 MPa. The flow rate of air inlet was 100 L min−1. In the presence of microbubbles colour of brine turned from transparent to milky. Direct agitation in brine was not used but gas–liquid mixing pump caused small flowing in the brine.

Cylindrical shape samples were weighed before brining to obtain the initial weight (M0). After brining, samples were weighed again (M1). Before brining the initial meat tissue consisted of dry matter and water (content of NaCl was negligible) thus the following equation was proposed:
(1)
M 0
=
m
drymatter , 0
+
m
water , 0

Where mdry matter,0 is the initial dry matter content and mwater,0 the initial water content of the meat. During brining NaCl penetrated into the meat sample, therefore weight of the sample:
(2)
M 1
=
m
NaCl
+
m
drymatter
+
m
water

Where M1 is the weight of the sample after brining, mNaCl is the weight of NaCl that was uptaken by the sample, mdry matter is the weight of dry matter and mwater the water content of the sample. Dry matter content was determined by drying at 105 °C until constant weight. The evaluation was performed for mNaCl/M0 and mwater/M0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of NaCl content and diffusion coefficient
ce:section.ce:para:
Determination of NaCl penetrated into the meat was carried out by Mohr method as described by Volpato et al.[27]. The NaCl content was measured from the 40 mm lenght center part of meat cylinders, 20 mm lenght end parts were cut down for the purpose that only the NaCl radial direction be determined. The diffusion coefficient of NaCl (D) was calculated from the concentration versus time data, which was fitted to Fick’s II law. The diffusion coefficient of NaCl was determined according to Telis et al.[28] (Eq. (3)):
(3)
C t
-
C 0
C
eq
-
C 0
= 1 -
∑
n = 0
∞
8
( 2 n + 1 )
2
π
2
e
- D
( 2 n + 1 )
2
π
2
t
L
2

where C0 the initial NaCl concentration in the meat sample (g 100 g−1), Ct is NaCl concentration in the meat sample (g 100 g−1) at given time t (s), Ceq is the equilibrium NaCl concentration (g 100 g−1) between brine and meat sample, D is the diffusion coefficient (m2 s−1), L is the radius of the cylinder shaped meat sample. The equilibrium NaCl concentration (Ceq) was calculated described by Eq. (4) [29].
(4)
C
eq
=
P
P +
0 , 75
×
C
brine
Where Ceq the equilibrium NaCl concentration (g 100 ml−1), P brine and meat weight ratio (dimensionless), Cbrine salt content of brine (g 100 ml−1).

To calculate D value an optimization problem was formulated. MS Excel 2016 tool SOLVER was used to solve the optimization with non-linear optimization method ,Generalized Reduced Gradient” by minimizing the sum of square errors (SSE) between experimental and calculated NaCl content. The exponential value in (Eq. (3)) is decreasing very quickly with increasing 'n', so the infinite sum was approximated by the first 50 member (n = 0…50), where the value was decreased by 4 order of magnitude at least. The diffusion model goodness of fit was calculated by R-square (R2) and Eq. (5) was used as general formula:
(5)
R
2
=
∑
(
Y p
-
Y -
p
)
2
∑
(
Y p
- Y )
2
+ ∑
(
Y p
-
Y -
p
)
2

where
Y p
is predicted value of Y ,
Y -
p
is average of predicted Y.

The water binding capacity (WBC) was measured according to the method of Graiver et al.[30]. It was calculated as the change in the water content of the meat (expressed as g 100 g−1) after soaking of the meat sample in the brine (mwater) as a function of the initial water content (mwater 0). The WBC was calculated using Eq. (6):
(6)
WBC =
m
water
-
m
w a t e r 0
m
w a t e r 0
x 100

The differential scanning calorimetric (DSC) measurements were performed in a Micro DSC II CS32 Calorimeter Vessels Setaram (Setaram Inc. Caluire-et-Cuire, France). Samples of 0.220 ± 0.005 g were obtained from the centre of each meat sample. The samples were placed in metal pans and hermetically closed. The heating rate of the scans was 1 °C min−1 from 25 to 90 °C. Distilled water was used as reference. Raw pork loin sample without brining was also tested to determine the denaturation peaks of main protein groups.

The 0.2 – 0.3 cm thick meat samples were used for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis. The samples were sectioned from center part of meat cylinders. The section plane was parallel to meat fibers. Samples for SEM were fixed for 24 h with glutaraldehyde (2.5 g 100 g−1) in a 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). After fixing ethanol dehydration, freeze-drying and sputter-coating were performed according to the procedure of Cao et al. [31]. SEM micrographs were taken by FEI Quanta 3D Two-Beam Scanning Electron Microscope (Hillsboro, OR, USA) at 5 °C, 700 Pa and 100% relative humidity. The 180 min brined samples were used for the SEM analysis.

Statistical analyses were performed for the results of mass balance, NaCl content, WBC and protein peak temperatures. The significant differences between brining methods were determined by SPSS 23.0 for Windows (Chicago, IL, USA) by analysis of variance (ANOVA). Differences were considered significant at p < 0.05 level. Homogenity of measured values was checked by Levene’s test (p < 0.05) and the significant differences between brining methods were analysed by Tukey HSD test (p < 0.05).

Fig. 2a shows the ratio between the mass of NaCl uptaken by the meat sample and the initial mass of the sample (mNaCl/M0) as a function of treatment time. Increasing mNaCl/M0 ratio was observed in case of all brining methods which is considered an absolute NaCl gain, due to the fact that the initial NaCl content of meat sample was negligible (0.1 g 100 g−1). Some internal factors e.g. temperature, pH, microstructure and external factors e.g. ultrasound can also affect the NaCl uptake[32]). Application of ultrasound alone significantly (p < 0.05) influenced the NaCl uptake from 150 min of brining. At 180 min of brining US and USMB meat samples showed 1.2 and 1.4 times higher mNaCl/M0 ratio than SB sample. Application of ultrasound in liquid media (e.g. brine) induces cavitation, temperature gradient, compression and decompression of material, generates microjets and microchannels on surfaces, which are responsible for the increased gain in NaCl. Comparing the brining methods, USMB brining resulted in significant higher mNaCl/M0 ratio (p < 0.05). The influence of ultrasound on mass transfer has been explained by several mechanisms. These include the asymmetric implosions of the cavitation bubbles close to a solid surface that generate microjets in the direction of the surface that can affect mass transfer by disturbing the boundary layer[33,34].

Changes in mass of water (mwater/M0) are shown in Fig. 2b . A slight decrease in mwater/M0 ratio was seen in all brining methods. During brining two-way mass transfer occur: NaCl penetrates to the meat from the brine due to diffusion and water migrates from the meat to the brine due to osmotic phenomena. When high-intensity ultrasound wave passes through a solid (e.g. through meat), it produces alternating expansion and compression, called ’sponge’ effect. The force generated by the ’sponge’ effect creates a microscopic channel and acts as a preferred channel for the outward diffusion of water molecules, increasing the effective water diffusivity[35,36]. Decrease in mwater/M0 ratio was quicker at US and USMB brining methods than SB. Ratio of mwater/M0 in case of US and USMB brinings were significantly lower than in SB from 15 min of brining. Cárcel et al. [2] have reported similar findings for pork loin (Longissimus dorsi) water mass transfer with 20 kHz, 100 W and 5.2 – 75.8 W cm−2 intensity. Gomez-Salazar et al. (2018) also found similar results, namely that 40 kHz, 110 W, 12.25 W cm−2 and 0–120 min ultrasonic marination of rabbit meat (leg) increased the weight loss, reduced water content and water holding capacity. The USMB brining resulted in lower mwater/M0 ratio than US brining but significant difference was not seen (p > 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NaCl content and determination of diffusion coefficient
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the changing in NaCl content of meat samples during brining. The brining methods had an effect on NaCl content of meat samples. When brining lasted for 90 min the NaCl content of US (8.6 ± 0.10 g NaCl 100 g−1) and USMB (9.5 ± 0.20 g NaCl 100 g−1) brined meat samples showed significantly higher values than SB samples (7.7 ± 0.26 g NaCl 100 g−1). Significant difference (p < 0.05) was also observed in NaCl content of US and USMB samples from 120 min of brining. Many reports have shown that ultrasound enhances penetration of low molecular weight drugs[37,38;23]) and NaCl into meat tissue during curing, salting or brining[2,4,7,39,40].

Fig. 4 presents the diffusion kinetics of NaCl into cylinder shaped pork loin samples as a function of different brining methods. Diffusion of NaCl into the meat samples followed an exponential pattern for all types of brining methods. A rapid increase in the NaCl concentration was observed in the first 30 min of brining (Fig. 3) then diffusion rate decreased thereafter. The rapid diffusion at the beginning of the brining can be attributed to the high concentration gradient between the 200 g L-1 NaCl brine and meat sample (0.1 g 100 g−1).

The diffusion of NaCl from brine into meat as muscle tissue is a complex process which is hard to described precisely by a simple unsteady unidirectional transport with constant diffusion coefficient because physical properties of meat are changing during the brining and curing process. For example, increasing NaCl concentration resulted in enlarged electrostatic repulsion between myofibrils, which then swell laterally and also resulted in osmotic dehydration[41]. Protein denaturation or diffusion of other components may also influence the NaCl diffusion in tissue[42]. In the present study several assumptions were made in order to modelling the diffusion process. The diffusion of NaCl into the meat was analyzed as a finite and unidirectional mass transport without any change of volume. The meat sample was considered as homogeneous and isothermal with initial conditions. Despite of simplifications a good agreement was observed between the predicted and measured data from Eq. (3) model (Fig. 4). This indicates that the used diffusion model describes precisely the NaCl diffusion kinetics during brining. The calculated D (m2 s−1) values are shown in Table 1 .

None of the samples reached equilibrium salt concentration but as it can be seen both ultrasound alone (US) and ultrasound combined with microbubbles produced in brine (USMB) increased the NaCl diffusion compared to static brined (SB) sample. Diffusion coefficients of US (DUS = 2.0 × 10-9 m2s−1) and USMB (DUSMB = 2.5 × 10-9 m2s−1) were an order of magnitude higher than SB (DSB = 1.8 × 10-10 m2s−1). However, between US and USMB brining methods significant difference was not observed in the diffusion coefficient.

Several studies can be found in which the authors studied the NaCl diffusion coefficient in pork meat during static and ultrasound assisted curing. But there are no results available for the case of ultrasound combined with microbubble in brine brining. However, the ultrasonic diffusion coefficient values found in the literature were consistent with ultrasonic brined data obtained in the present work. From earlier studies, for example curing of pork Longissimus dorsi in 180 g L-1 brine, 2.2 × 10-10 m2s−1 NaCl diffusion coefficient was reported by Fox [43] and Dussap et al. [44] have found similar result in their work in which diffusion coefficient was 2,2 × 10-10 m2s−1. Graiver et al. [30] examined the effect of 5–200 g L-1 NaCl concentrations on diffusion coefficient in pork Longissimus dorsi and found D = 5 × 10-10 m2s−1 value at the highest concentration. Siró et al. [4] also studied the effects of ultrasound on pork Longissimus dorsi slabs and obtained 2,4 × 10-10 m2s−1 diffusion coefficient in case of static brining and 3.1 × 10-9 – 4.7 × 10-10 m2s−1 coefficients for samples which were ultrasonicated in 40 g L-1 NaCl brine in 2 – 4 W m−2 intensity range.
Fig. 5 shows the calculated values of water binding capacity (WBC) (Eq. (6)) as a function of brining time in case of different brining methods.

In the first 15 min of brining increasing WBC was seen for all brining methods. Increase of water-binding capacity is explained by the effect of penetrated NaCl. The Na+ and Cl- ions connect to –COO– and –NH3 side chains of myofibers and form an ion ,cloud” around the filaments. The same charged ion clouds result in repulsive forces between the myofibers that increase the distance among them causing the filament lattice to swell. The swelling provides higher volume and higher number of protein side chains to bind water[45,46].

From 30 min of brining on a firm decrease in WBC was observed for all brining methods and as the brining progressed WBC showed negative values. This can be interpreted as meaning that the water content of meat sample (mwater) is lower than the initial water content (mwater0) suggesting dehydration. It can be related to the salting-out phenomenon. When more and more Na+ and Cl- ions penetrate into the meat a competition occur between the ions and proteins to interact with water molecules. The ions interact with water molecules that result in fewer water molecules available for forming protein-water bonds. Thus protein–protein interactions become more frequent and the protein molecules are forming hydrophobic bonds with each other. As a result, proteins are dehydrated and release the water therefore the water binding capacity of the meat decreases. Akse et al.[47]found that fish salting-out was observed above 90 – 110 g L-1 salt concentrations and Graiver et al. [30]had similar findings above 100 g L-1 in pork Longissimus dorsi. The USMB brined sample showed significantly quicker decrease in WBC (p < 0.05) than SB and US samples. Some authors reported the ultrasonication alone resulted in reduced WBC in rabbit meat (Gomez-Salazar et al. 2018) and beef semitendinosus[48]. Decreasing WBC can be advantageous e.g. in the manufacture of dry hams because it can speed up the drying process.

In case of raw meat without any brining used in our experiment three thermal transitions were detected with endothermic peak (Tmax) values at 58.7 °C, 66.8 °C and 80.1 °C. These correspond to myosin, sarcoplasmic proteins and collagen and actin. In our experiment there were no peaks representing myosin and sarcoplasmic proteins at 15 min of brining. The loss of denaturation peaks suggests that protein – in particular myosin – denaturation has already taken place in case of SB, US and USMB brinings. Quinn et al. [49]) were the first to demonstrate that the denaturation peak of myosin and actin were decreased from 59 °C to 57.5 °C and from 81 °C to 74.5 °C, upon the addition of 0.23 M NaCl (1.33% w/w) to pork tissue. At higher NaCl concentrations the peaks were not detectable. Even the low salt concetrations cause the protein filament lattice to expand. This expansion weakens the bond between the myosin and F-actin, thus reducing denaturation temperature [49,30] . The actin peaks were observed for SB sample up to 90 min and for US and USMB up to 60 min of brining. In general actin peak can be found around 80 °C in pork meat[49,50,51,52] . As Table 2 presents, actin denaturation temperature (Tmax, actin) of brined sample showed slight decreasing tendency with increasing brining time, independently of the brining methods. The USMB sample showed significantly (p < 0.05) lower Tmax, actin average value than SB sample. This is probably due to the combination of increased NaCl penetration caused chemical competition between ions, water molecules and myofibers coupled with actions of ultrasound and microbubbles. Some authors have reported similar findings about the effect of ultrasound on the decrease of actin denaturation temperature [4,52]. Ultrasound combined with mircobubbles resulted in samples with Tmax, actin values lower on the average by 0.5 °C than ultrasound alone (p > 0.05). The cavitation caused by ultrasound combined with microbubbles generated in brine results in an enhanced NaCl penetration that leads to the change in denaturation temperature of actin.

SEM analysis was performed to reveal the microstructural changes in meat tissue. For the SEM imaging 180-minute brined samples were used. As presented in Fig. 6 ultrasonication and ultrasonication combined with microbubbles caused microstructural changes in the meat tissue.

Increase in thickness of fibers was seen due to ultrasonic treatments compared to static brining. The average thickness of SB sample filaments was 29,0 ± 2,9 µm (Fig. 6a). In general, the thickness of filaments increased in both US and USMB brining methods (Fig. 6b and 6c). The highest fiber thickness 40,2 ± 7,0 µm was found in USMB brining method (Fig. 6c). The US brining resulted also an increased thickness of 38,1 ± 2,1 µm. The change in microstructure is due to the accompanying phenomenon of ultrasound induced cavitation which supports the charging of filaments with Na+ and Cl- ions. According to Kang et al. [53]and Wang et al.[54]cavitation may also have an effect on the nebulin network which perpendicularly stabilizes the meat fibers. On the micrographs showing a sample treated by ultrasound combined with microbubbles (Fig. 6c) small pores can be observed on the fiber’s surface with diameters of approximately 2–3 µm. On the surface of SB and US sample pores can not be seen thereby pores may presumably be the result of injuries caused by the explosion of microbubbles. Meijering et al.[55] had similar findings in the field of ultrasound and microbubble-targeted delivery of macromolecules into cells. Their electron microscopy results showed pore formation called ’sonoporation’ pores on cells surface after ultrasonic treatments without significantly changing the cell morphology. Sonoporation pores created by ultrasound combined with microbubbles on meat cell surface can be considered as technologically advantageous e.g. in the manufacture of dry ham. Disrupted meat microstructure enhances the water release and drying of meat, increases the chewability that results in easier digestibility.

Many reports have already shown ultrasound is a promising method to increase NaCl diffusion in meat and can be used in meat industry for accelerating brining process and enhancing mass transfer. Therefore in this study the effect of ultrasound and gas microbubbles during pork meat brining was evaluated.

Our results indicated that both ultrasound and combination of ultrasound and microbubbles caused microstructural changes in pork meat tissue. In the case of the latter, pores were formed on the meat fibers surface. It seems that these pores considerably influenced other characteristics of meat such as mass transfer, NaCl uptake and diffusion and water binding capacity.

Ultrasound combined with gas microbubbles in brine resulted the highest NaCl diffusivity. The NaCl diffusion into the meat tissue followed exponential kinetics in case of all brining methods. Diffusion coefficients were calculated from a mathematical model assuming unidirectional mass transfer and constant diffusion coefficient during the brining process. Good agreements were observed between measured and calculated data indicating the applicability of the model. Combination of ultrasound and microbubbles resulted in more enhanced NaCl uptake and water loss than ultrasound alone or static brining. Similar result could be observed for water binding capacity. The enhanced NaCl penetration led to the quick change of myosin that was not detectable.

Effect of the combination of ultrasound and microbubbles can be considered advantageous for example in dry-cured ham production where the main purpose is to remove water from meat in the drying-ripening process.

Anna Visy: Writing - original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Gábor Jónás: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Dávid Szakos: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zsuzsanna Horváth- Mezőfi: Visualization. Karina Ilona Hidas: Investigation. Annamária Barkó: Visualization. László Friedrich: Project administration, Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interest or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The project was supported by the Doctoral School of Food Science (Hungarian University of Agriculture and Life Sciences) and by the European Union and co-financed by the European Social Fund (grant agreement no. EFOP-3.6.3-VEKOP-16-2017-00005) and Supported by the ÚNKP-20-4-I New National Excellence Program of the Ministry for Innovation and Technology from the Source of the National Research, Development and Innovation Fund.
